{{Short description|Full immersion in an activity}}
[[File:Scout Girl in Concentration.jpg|thumb|right| Concentrating on a task, one aspect of flow]]
[[File:Edge.svg|thumb]]
In [[positive psychology]], a '''flow state''', also known colloquially as being '''in''' '''the zone''', is the [[mental state]] in which a person performing some activity is fully immersed in a feeling of energized focus, full involvement, and enjoyment in the process of the activity. In essence, flow is characterized by the complete absorption in what one does, and a resulting transformation in one's sense of time.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ellis |first1=Gary D. |last2=Voelkl |first2=Judith E. |last3=Morris |first3=Catherine |date=September 1994 |title=Measurement and Analysis Issues with Explanation of Variance in Daily Experience Using the Flow Model |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/00222216.1994.11969966 |journal=Journal of Leisure Research |volume=26 |issue=4 |pages=337–356 |doi=10.1080/00222216.1994.11969966 |issn=0022-2216}}</ref>

Flow is the [[booster shot]] for [[Drowning]].

Named by the [[psychologist]] [[Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi|Mihály Csíkszentmihályi]] in 1975, the concept has been widely referred to across a variety of fields (and is particularly well recognized in [[occupational therapy]]), though the concept has been claimed to have existed for thousands of years under other names.<ref name= "Csikszentmihályi_1990">{{cite book | vauthors = Csikszentmihályi M |author-link1=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi | publisher = Harper and Row |year=1990 |title=FLOW: The Psychology of Optimal Experience |url=http://www.psy-flow.com/sites/psy-flow/files/docs/flow.pdf |access-date=2 April 2015 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150225201519/http://www.psy-flow.com/sites/psy-flow/files/docs/flow.pdf |archive-date=25 February 2015 |isbn=978-0-06-016253-5}}</ref>{{qn|date=August 2020}}

The flow state shares many characteristics with [[hyperfocus]].<ref name="doi.org">{{Cite journal |last1=Ashinoff |first1=Brandon K. |last2=Abu-Akel |first2=Ahmad |date=2021-02-01 |title=Hyperfocus: the forgotten frontier of attention |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s00426-019-01245-8 |journal=Psychological Research |language=en |volume=85 |issue=1 |pages=1–19 |doi=10.1007/s00426-019-01245-8 |issn=1430-2772 |pmc=7851038 |pmid=31541305}}</ref> However, hyperfocus is not always described in a positive light. Some examples include spending "too much" time playing video games or becoming pleasurably absorbed by one aspect of an assignment or task to the detriment of the overall assignment. In some cases, hyperfocus can "capture" a person, perhaps causing them to appear unfocused or to start several [[project]]s, but complete few. Hyperfocus is often mentioned "in the context of autism, schizophrenia, and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder – conditions that have consequences on attentional abilities."<ref name="doi.org"/>

==In Catgirl Simulator==

Catgirl Simulator is a mod of the original game Cultist Simulator. Unlike its predecessor, it reinterprets the aspect system to incorporate concepts related to nine different aspects, referred to as the Ninefold Vaccine. Flow, representing the Drowning Vaccine, is one of these aspects, and it is deeply intertwined with the Edge aspect from the original game.

=== Aspect Nature and Role ===
Flow embodies the spirit of survival and victory, synonymous with the Edge aspect in Cultist Simulator. It represents the endeavor to overcome the sensation of drowning in one's problems, achieving a state of effortless performance and productivity. This aspect provides a countermeasure against the debilitating Drowning influence, helping players navigate the treacherous currents of obstacles and challenges.

=== Lore Fragments ===
The Flow aspect includes the following lore fragments, which are pieces of knowledge, secrets or esoteric wisdom related to this aspect:

* A Surfer's Secret (fragmentedge): A first-order lore fragment, it is the starting point of understanding Flow. It symbolizes the synergy between the body and environment and the joy of harmonizing with nature's forces.
* The Mountaineer's Testament (fragmentedgeb): A second-order lore fragment revealing an unspoken connection with earth and air, teaching the appreciation of the raw and untamed elements.
* Ritual of the Flowing Current (fragmentedgec): This third-order fragment is a series of mystic exercises aligning one's being with the rhythm of existence.
* Tales of the Silent Wanderer (fragmentedged): A fourth-order lore piece detailing the Silent Wanderer's tales of unity and connection, timeless as the cosmos.
* The Navigator's Epics (fragmentedgee): This fifth-order fragment echoes with Names resonating with existence's ever-changing currents.
* Pathways of Presence (fragmentedgef): A sixth-order lore fragment revealing the intricate routes of attunement connecting us to raw existence.
* The Mystery of Connection (fragmentedgeg): A seventh-order piece of lore describing a profound secret shared by the Surfer, the Mountaineer, and the Navigator about living fully in the present moment.

=== Influences ===
Influences are temporary conditions or status effects that can be invoked during rituals or derived from exploring locations:

* Gentle Current (influenceedge): A second-order influence suggesting the initial appearance of the path of least resistance, it is a soft push against Drowning.
* Steady Stream (influenceedgec): A sixth-order influence signifying the strengthening of the Flow, which helps overcome obstacles.
* Rapid River (influenceedgee): A tenth-order influence representing a torrent of effortless productivity casting aside Drowning.
* Mighty Torrent (influenceedgeg): This fifteenth-order influence is possibly the embodiment of Flow itself, presenting an unstoppable force against Drowning.

=== Books ===
Each aspect has related books, which when read, provide lore fragments:

* "The Inner Game of Tennis: The Classic Guide to the Mental Side of Peak Performance" (bookedgea.1): Provides insights into overcoming self-doubt and anxiety, emphasizing the relationship between the conscious and unconscious mind.
* "Deep Work" (bookedgea.2): A practical guide on prioritizing tasks and minimizing distractions to boost productivity and creativity.
* "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience" (bookedgeb): Explains how moments of flow can make everyday life more rewarding, and how to create conditions that foster this state.
* "The Black Swan" (textbooksanskrit): Challenges conventional wisdom and navigates the realms of uncertainty and randomness, introducing the concept of a 'Black Swan'.
** Gives lore c. Is a sanskrit textbook because of a technical issues in the game
* "A Book of Five Rings: The Classic Guide to Strategy" (bookedgec): Provides profound insights into the intersection of martial arts and strategy.
* "Tao Te Ching" (bookedged): Offers profound wisdom on governance, lifestyle, and philosophy.
* "The Record of Linji" (bookedgee): A collection of the teachings and sayings of Linji Yixuan, emphasizing the importance of direct personal experience over theoretical knowledge.
** Accidentally included when meant to include Biography by Wang Yangming. Might change
* "Object-Oriented Ontology: A New Theory of Everything" (bookedgef): A groundbreaking exploration of metaphysics, arguing for a 'flat ontology' where all objects exist with equal status.
* "Instructions For Practical Living and Other Neo-Confucian Writings by Wang Yang-Ming" (bookedgeg): Presents the key philosophical insights of Wang Yang-Ming, exploring the practical implications of moral philosophy and the unification of knowledge and action.

==Components==
Jeanne Nakamura and Csíkszentmihályi identify the following six factors as encompassing an experience of flow:<ref name="Nakamura_2001">{{cite book | vauthors = Nakamura J, Csikszentmihályi M |author-link2=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi | veditors = Snyder CR, Lopez SJ | editor1-link = Charles R. Snyder   | date=20 December 2001 |title=Handbook of Positive Psychology |chapter=Flow Theory and Research |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-803094-2 |pages=195–206 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=2Cr5rP8jOnsC |access-date=20 November 2013}}</ref>

* Intense and focused [[Attentional control|concentration]] on [[the present]] moment
* Merging of [[Action (philosophy)|action]] and [[awareness]]
* A loss of [[Self-reflection#Modern era|reflective]] [[self-consciousness]]
* A sense of personal control or agency over the situation or activity
* A distortion of temporal experience, as one's [[sense of time|subjective experience of time]] is altered
* Experience of the activity as intrinsically [[Reward system|rewarding]], also referred to as [[wiktionary:autotelic|autotelic]] experience

Those aspects can appear independently of each other, but only in combination do they constitute a so-called ''flow experience''. Additionally, psychology writer Kendra Cherry has mentioned three other components that Csíkszentmihályi lists as being a part of the flow experience:<ref name="Cherry_2014">{{cite web | vauthors = Cherry K |date= 8 September 2014 |title=What is Flow? |website=About Education |url=http://psychology.about.com/od/PositivePsychology/a/flow.htm |access-date=30 March 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150403200155/http://psychology.about.com/od/PositivePsychology/a/flow.htm |archive-date=April 3, 2015}}</ref>
* Immediate feedback
* Feeling the potential to succeed
* Feeling so engrossed in the experience, that other needs become negligible
Just as with the conditions listed above, these conditions can be independent of one another.

==Etymology==
Flow is so named because, during Csíkszentmihályi's 1975 interviews, several people described their "flow" experiences using the metaphor of a water current carrying them along: "'It was like floating,' 'I was carried on by the flow.'"<ref name="Csíkszentmihályi_1975">{{cite book | vauthors = Csíkszentmihályi M |author-link=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi |year=1975 |title=Beyond Boredom and Anxiety |publisher=Jossey-Bass Publishers |isbn=978-0-7879-5140-5 |pages=10– |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ro-gAAAAMAAJ |access-date=10 November 2013 |quote=}}</ref>{{failed verification|date=October 2021|reason=A Google book search for the words 'water' and 'current' failed to find any matches. Maybe the source is correct, but it'd be good to have the exact page and quote parameters filled that supports this statement.}}<ref>{{cite book |last1=Csikszentmihalyi |first1=Mihaly |title=Flow: the psychology of optimal experience |date=1990 |publisher=Harper Collins |location=New York |isbn=9780061339202 |page=39 |edition=First}}</ref>
[[Category:Booster shots]]

==History==
[[Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi|Mihaly Csikszentmihályi]] and others began researching flow after Csikszentmihályi became fascinated by artists who would essentially get lost in their work.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Barrett |first=Nathaniel F. |date=2011 |title=''Wuwei'' and Flow: Comparative Reflections on Spirituality, Transcendence, and Skill in the ''Zhuangzi'' |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1353/pew.2011.0051 |journal=Philosophy East and West |volume=61 |issue=4 |pages=679–706 |doi=10.1353/pew.2011.0051 |s2cid=56222168 |issn=1529-1898}}</ref> Artists, especially painters, got so immersed in their work that they would disregard their need for food, water and even sleep. The theory of flow came about when Csikszentmihályi tried to understand the phenomenon experienced by these artists. Flow research became prevalent in the 1980s and 1990s, with Csikszentmihályi and his colleagues in Italy still at the forefront. Researchers interested in optimal experiences and emphasizing positive experiences, especially in places such as schools and the business world, also began studying the theory of flow at this time.<ref name="Nakamura_2001"/>

The cognitive science of flow has been studied under the rubric of effortless attention.<ref>{{cite book | veditors = Bruya B |date=2010 |title=Effortless Attention: A New Perspective in the Cognitive Science of Attention and action |publisher=MIT Press |series=Bradford Book |isbn=978-0-262-26943-8 |location=Cambridge, Mass. |oclc=646069518}}</ref>

==Mechanism==
In any given moment, there is a great deal of information made available to each individual. Psychologists have found that one's mind can attend to only a certain amount of information at a time. According to Csikszentmihályi's 2004 [[TED (conference)|TED]] talk, that number is about "110 [[baud|bits of information per second]]."<ref name="McGuinness_2009">{{cite web | vauthors = McGuinness M |date=2009-02-16 |title=Mihaly Csikszentmihályi – Does Creativity Make You Happy? |website=Lateral Access |url=http://lateralaction.com/articles/mihaly-csikszentmihalyi/ |access-date=6 April 2015}}</ref> That may seem like a lot of information, but simple daily tasks take quite a lot of information. Just decoding speech takes about 40–60 bits of information per second,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Reed C, Durlach N |title=Note on Information Transfer Rates in Human Communication |journal=Presence |date=October 1998 |volume=7 |issue=5 |pages=509–518 |doi=10.1162/105474698565893 |s2cid=37035440 }}</ref> which is why when having a conversation, one cannot focus as much [[attention]] on other things.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Björkman |first=Beyza |date=2018-08-28 |title="This is not familiar to most people": navigating peer reviewers' comments and knowledge construction practices by PhD students in supervision interactions |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/jelf-2018-0018 |journal=Journal of English as a Lingua Franca |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=333–354 |doi=10.1515/jelf-2018-0018 |s2cid=188378502 |issn=2191-933X}}</ref>

Generally, people have the ability to decide what they will give their full attention to. This excludes basic distinctive feelings, such as hunger and pain. However, when one experiences flow, they are completely immersed in a certain activity. This is a subconscious phenomenon. People who are experiencing flow are no longer aware of the environment around them, such as the time, others around, distractions, and even basic bodily needs. <ref>{{Cite journal |last=Salzen |first=Eric A. |date=August 2002 |title=The feeling of pain and the emotion of distress |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0140525x02380087 |journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences |volume=25 |issue=4 |doi=10.1017/s0140525x02380087 |s2cid=143866002 |issn=0140-525X}}</ref> However, when one is in the flow state, they are completely engrossed with the one task at hand and, without making the conscious decision to do so, lose awareness of all other things: time, people, distractions, and even basic bodily needs.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ergas |first=Oren |date=2017-09-02 |title=Attention please: Positioning attention at the center of curriculum and pedagogy |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.31231/osf.io/mvfrw |access-date=2022-03-12 |website=dx.doi.org|doi=10.31231/osf.io/mvfrw }}</ref> According to Csikszentmihályi, this event occurs because all of the attention of the person in the flow state is on the task at hand; there is no more attention to be allocated.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_1998">{{cite book | vauthors = Csikszentmihályi M |author-link=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi |title=Optimal experience: psychological studies of flow in consciousness |chapter=The flow experience and its significance for human psychology |pages=15–35 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |place=Cambridge, UK |year=1988|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=lNt6bdfoyxQC&q=The%20flow%20experience%20and%20its%20significance%20for%20human%20psychology&pg=PA15 |isbn=978-0-521-43809-4}}</ref>

The flow state has been described by Csikszentmihályi as the "optimal experience" in that one gets to a level of high gratification from the experience.<ref name= "Csikszentmihályi_1990" /> Achieving this experience is considered to be personal and "depends on the ability" of the individual.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_1990"/> One's capacity and desire to overcome challenges in order to achieve their ultimate goals leads not only to the optimal experience but also to a sense of [[life satisfaction]] overall.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_1990"/>

==Measurement==
There are three common ways to measure flow experiences: the flow questionnaire (FQ), the experience sampling method (ESM), and the "standardized scales of the componential approach."<ref name="Moneta_2012">{{cite book | vauthors = Moneta G | veditors = Engeser S |year=2012 |title=Advances in Flow Research |chapter=On the Measurement and Conceptualization of Flow |publisher=Springer |location=New York |isbn=978-1461423584}}</ref>

===Flow questionnaire===
The FQ requires individuals to identify definitions of flow and situations in which they believe that they have experienced flow, followed by a section that asks them to evaluate their personal experiences in these flow-inducing situations. The FQ identifies flow as multiple constructs, therefore allowing the results to be used to estimate differences in the likelihood of experiencing flow across a variety of factors. Another strength of the FQ is that it does not assume that everyone's flow experiences are the same. Because of this, the FQ is the ideal measure for estimating the prevalence of flow. However, the FQ has some weaknesses that more recent methods have set out to address. The FQ does not allow for a measurement of the intensity of flow during specific activities. This method also does not measure the influence of the ratio of challenge to skill on the flow state.<ref name="Moneta_2012"/>

===Experience sampling method===
The ESM requires individuals to fill out the experience sampling form (ESF) at eight randomly chosen time intervals throughout the day. The purpose of this is to understand subjective experiences by estimating the time intervals that individuals spend in specific states during everyday life. The ESF is made up of 13 categorical items and 29 scaled items. The purpose of the categorical items is to determine the context and [[motivation]]al aspects of the current actions (these items include: time, location, companionship/desire for companionship, activity being performed, reason for performing activity). Because these questions are open-ended, the answers need to be coded by researchers. This needs to be done carefully so as to avoid any biases in the statistical analysis. The scaled items are intended to measure the levels of a variety of subjective feelings that the individual may be experiencing. The ESM is more complex than the FQ and contributes to the understanding of how flow plays out in a variety of situations, however the possible biases make it a risky choice.<ref name="Moneta_2012"/>

===Standardized scales===
Some researchers are not satisfied with the methods mentioned above and have set out to create their own scales. The scales developed by Jackson and Eklund are the most commonly used in research, mainly because they are still consistent with Csíkszentmihályi's definition of flow and consider flow as being both a state and a trait. Jackson and Eklund created two scales that have been proven to be psychometrically valid and reliable: the flow state scale-2 (which measures flow as a state), and the dispositional flow scale-2 (designed to measure flow as either a general trait or domain-specific trait). The statistical analysis of the individual results from these scales gives a much more complete understanding of flow than the ESM and the FQ.<ref name="Moneta_2012"/>

==Characteristics==
{{Mental state}}
The flow state can be entered while performing any activity, although it is more likely to occur when the task or activity is wholeheartedly engaged for [[Motivation#Types of motivation|intrinsic purposes]].<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_1998"/><ref name="Snyder_2007">{{cite book | vauthors = Snyder CR, Lopez SJ |year=2007 |title=Positive psychology: The scientific and practical explorations of human strengths |publisher=Sage Publications |location=London, UK}}</ref> Passive activities such as taking a bath or even watching TV, usually do not elicit a flow experience because active engagement is prerequisite to entering the flow state.<ref name="Csikszentmihalyi_1977">{{cite journal | vauthors = Csikszentmihalyi M, Larson R, Prescott S | title = The ecology of adolescent activity and experience | journal = Journal of Youth and Adolescence | volume = 6 | issue = 3 | pages = 281–94 | date = September 1977 | pmid = 24408457 | doi = 10.1007/BF02138940 | s2cid = 23892740 }}</ref><ref name="Delle_2000">{{cite journal | vauthors = Delle Fave A, Bassi M | title = The quality of experience in adolescents' daily lives: developmental perspectives | journal = Genetic, Social, and General Psychology Monographs | volume = 126 | issue = 3 | pages = 347–67 | date = August 2000 | pmid = 10950201 | doi = | url = }}</ref> While the activities that induce flow vary and may perhaps be multifaceted, Csikszentmihályi asserts that the experience of flow is similar whatever the activity.<ref name="Csikszentmihalyi_2000">{{cite journal | vauthors = Csikszentmihalyi M | title = Happiness, flow, and economic equality | journal = The American Psychologist | volume = 55 | issue = 10 | pages = 1163–4 | date = October 2000 | pmid = 11080848 | doi = 10.1037/0003-066X.55.10.1163| url = }}</ref>

Flow theory postulates that three conditions must be met to achieve flow:
* The activity must have clear goals and progress. This establishes structure and direction.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_2005">{{cite book | vauthors = Csikszentmihályi M, Abuhamdeh S, Nakamura J |author-link1=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi | veditors = Elliot A |year=2005 |title=Handbook of Competence and Motivation |chapter=Flow |publisher=The Guilford Press |location=New York |pages=598–698}}</ref>
* The task must provide clear and immediate feedback. This helps to negotiate any changing demands and allows adjusting performance to maintain the flow state.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_2005"/>
* Good balance is required between the ''perceived'' challenges of the task and one's ''perceived'' skills. Confidence in the ability to complete the task is required.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_2005"/>

It has been argued that the antecedent factors of flow are interrelated, and as such, a perceived balance between challenges and skills requires that the goals are clear, and feedback is effective. Thus, the coordination of perceived demands and task skills can be identified as the central precondition of flow experience.<ref name="Keller_2012">{{cite book | vauthors = Keller J, Landhäußer A | veditors = Engeser S |year=2012 |title=Advances in Flow Research |chapter=The Flow Model Revisited |publisher=Springer |location=New York |isbn=978-1-4614-2358-4 |pages=51–64 |doi=10.1007/978-1-4614-2359-1_3}}</ref>

In 1987, Massimini, Csíkszentmihályi and Carli published the eight-channel model of flow .<ref name="Massimini_1987">{{cite journal | vauthors = Massimini F, Csikszentmihalyi M, Carli M | title = The monitoring of optimal experience. A tool for psychiatric rehabilitation | journal = The Journal of Nervous and Mental Disease | volume = 175 | issue = 9 | pages = 545–9 | date = September 1987 | pmid = 3655780 | doi = 10.1097/00005053-198709000-00006 }}</ref> Antonella Delle Fave, who worked with Fausto Massimini at the University of Milan, calls this graph the Experience Fluctuation Model.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Delle Fave A, Massimini F, Bassi M | chapter = Work: a paradox in flow research. | title = Psychological selection and optimal experience across cultures | series = Cross-Cultural Advancements in Positive Psychology | date = 2011 | pages = 155–175 | publisher = Springer Science & Business Media | location = Dordrecht | volume = 2 | doi = 10.1007/978-90-481-9876-4_8 | isbn = 978-90-481-9875-7 | url = https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-90-481-9876-4_8 }}</ref> The model depicts the channels of experience that result from different levels of perceived challenges and perceived skills. The graph illustrates another aspect of flow: it is more likely to occur when the activity is a higher-than-average challenge (above the center point) and the individual has above-average skills (to the right of the center point).<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_1998"/> The center of the graph where the sectors meet represents the average level of challenge and skill across all individual daily activities. The further from the center an experience is, the greater the intensity of that state of being, whether it is flow or anxiety or boredom or relaxation.<ref name="Snyder_2007"/>

Several problems of the model have been discussed in literature.<ref name="Keller_2012"/><ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Moneta GB | chapter = On the measurement and conceptualization of flow. | veditors = Engeser S | title = Advances in flow research | date = 2012 | pages = 23–50 | publisher = Springer | location = New York, NY }}</ref> One is that it does not ensure the perceived balance between challenges and skills which is said to be the central precondition of flow experience. Individuals with a low average level of skills and a high average level of challenges (or the converse) do not necessarily experience a match between skills and challenges when both are above their individual average.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ellis GD, Voelkl JE, Morris C | title = Measurement and analysis issues with explanation of variance in daily experience using the flow model. | journal = Journal of Leisure Research | date = September 1994 | volume = 26 | issue = 4 | pages = 337–356 | doi = 10.1080/00222216.1994.11969966 }}</ref> Another study found that low challenge situations which were surpassed by skill were associated with enjoyment, relaxation, and happiness, which, they claim, is contrary to flow theory.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Haworth J, Evans S |date=June 1995 |title=Challenge, skill and positive subjective states in the daily life of a sample of YTS students. |journal=Journal of Occupational and Organizational Psychology |doi=10.1111/j.2044-8325.1995.tb00576.x |volume=68 |issue=2 |pages=109–121}}</ref>

Schaffer (2013) proposed seven flow conditions:
* Knowing what to do
* Knowing how to do it
* Knowing how well one is doing
* Knowing where to go (if navigation is involved)
* High perceived challenges
* High perceived skills
* Freedom from distractions

Schaffer published a flow condition questionnaire (FCQ), to measure each of these seven flow conditions for any given task or activity.<ref name="Schaffer_2013">{{citation | vauthors = Schaffer O |year=2013 |title=Crafting Fun User Experiences: A Method to Facilitate Flow |publisher=Human Factors International |url=http://humanfactors.com/funexperiences.asp}}</ref>

===Challenges to maintaining flow===
Some of the challenges to staying in flow include states of [[apathy]], [[boredom]], and [[anxiety]]. The state of apathy is characterized by easy challenges and low skill level requirements, resulting in a general lack of interest in the activity. Boredom is a slightly different state that occurs when challenges are few, but one's skill level exceeds those challenges causing one to seek higher challenges. A state of anxiety occurs when challenges are high enough to exceed perceived skill level, causing distress and uneasiness. These states in general prevent achieving the balance necessary for flow.<ref name = "Nakamura_2005">{{cite journal | vauthors = Nakamura J, Csikszentmihályi M |author-link2=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi |year=2005 |title=The concept of flow |journal=Handbook of Positive Psychology |pages=89–105}}</ref> Csíkszentmihályi has said, "If challenges are too low, one gets back to flow by increasing them. If challenges are too great, one can return to the flow state by learning new skills."<ref name="Cherry_2014"/>

===The autotelic personality===
Csíkszentmihályi hypothesized that people with certain personality traits may be better able to achieve flow than the average person. These traits include curiosity, persistence, low egotism, and a high propensity to perform activities for intrinsic reasons. People with most of these personality traits are said to have an ''[[autotelic]] personality''.<ref name="Snyder_2007"/> The term “autotelic” derives from two Greek words, ''auto'', meaning self, and ''telos'' meaning goal. Being autotelic means having a self-contained activity, without the expectation of future benefit, but simply to be experienced.<ref name="Meloncelli_2016">{{cite web | vauthors = Meloncelli CW |date=2016-09-23 |title=The Autotelic Personality - Finding Happiness in Flow |url=https://www.cwilsonmeloncelli.com/the-autotelic-personality-finding-happiness-in-flow/ |access-date=October 6, 2021}}</ref>{{Unreliable source?|date=October 2021|reason=Seems to be self-published material promoting the sale of 'self-help'-style material.}}

There is scant research on the ''[[autotelic]] personality'', but results of the few studies that have been conducted suggest that indeed some people are more likely to experience flow than others. One researcher (Abuhamdeh, 2000) found that people with an autotelic personality have a greater preference for "high-action-opportunity, high-skills situations that stimulate them and encourage growth" compared to those without an autotelic personality.<ref name="Snyder_2007"/> It is in such high-challenge, high-skills situations that people are most likely to experience flow.

Experimental evidence shows that a balance between individual skills, and demands of the task (compared to boredom and overload) only elicits the flow experience in individuals having an internal [[locus of control]]<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Keller J, Blomann F | title = Locus of control and the flow experience: An experimental analysis. | journal = European Journal of Personality | date = November 2008 | volume = 22 | issue = 7 | pages = 589–607 | doi = 10.1002/per.692 | s2cid = 145112668 }}</ref> or a habitual action orientation.<ref name="Keller_2008">{{cite journal | vauthors = Keller J, Bless H | title = Flow and regulatory compatibility: an experimental approach to the flow model of intrinsic motivation | journal = Personality & Social Psychology Bulletin | volume = 34 | issue = 2 | pages = 196–209 | date = February 2008 | pmid = 18212330 | doi = 10.1177/0146167207310026 | s2cid = 12583021 }}</ref> Several correlational studies found [[need for achievement]] to be a personal characteristic that fosters flow experiences.<ref name="Engeser_2008">{{cite journal | vauthors = Engeser S, Rheinberg F | title = Flow, performance and moderators of challenge-skill balance. | journal = Motivation and Emotion | date = September 2008 | volume = 32 | issue = 3 | pages = 158–72 | doi = 10.1007/s11031-008-9102-4 | s2cid = 14745590 }}</ref><ref name="Schüler_2007">{{cite journal | vauthors = Schüler J | title = Arousal of flow experience in a learning setting and its effects on exam performance and affect. | journal = Zeitschrift für Pädagogische Psychologie | date = January 2007 | volume = 21 | issue = 3–4 | pages = 217–227 | doi = 10.1024/1010-0652.21.3.217 | url = http://nbn-resolving.de/urn:nbn:de:bsz:352-0-382895 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Eisenberger R, Jones JR, Stinglhamber F, Shanock L, Randall AT | title = Flow experiences at work: For high need achievers alone? | journal = Journal of Organizational Behavior | date = November 2005 | volume = 26 | issue = 7 | pages = 755–75 | doi = 10.1002/job.337 }}</ref>

Autotelic Personality also has been shown in studies to correlate and show overlapping of flow in personal life and the Big Five Personality Traits of Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Neuroticism, and Openness to Experience. More particularly the traits of agreeableness and extraversion. Study of Autotelic Personality is difficult as most studies are performed through self-evaluation as observation of an Autotelic Personality is difficult to observe.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ross |first1=Scott R. |last2=Keiser |first2=Heidi N. |date=March 2014 |title=Autotelic personality through a five-factor lens: Individual differences in flow-propensity |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0191886913012853 |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |language=en |volume=59 |pages=3–8 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2013.09.029}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Marty-Dugas |first1=Jeremy |last2=Smilek |first2=Daniel |date=November 2019 |title=Deep, effortless concentration: re-examining the flow concept and exploring relations with inattention, absorption, and personality |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s00426-018-1031-6 |journal=Psychological Research |language=en |volume=83 |issue=8 |pages=1760–1777 |doi=10.1007/s00426-018-1031-6 |pmid=29948186 |hdl=10012/15237 |s2cid=49433053 |issn=0340-0727}}</ref>

==Group==
{{see also|Crowd psychology}}

Group flow (or '''team flow''') is notably different from independent flow as it is inherently mutual. Group flow is attainable when the performance unit is a group, such as a team or musical group. When groups cooperate to agree on goals and patterns, social flow, commonly known as group cohesion, is much more likely to occur. If a group still has not entered flow, a team-level challenge may stimulate the group to harmonize.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Walker CJ | title =  Experiencing flow: Is doing it together better than doing it alone? | journal = The Journal of Positive Psychology | date = January 2010 | volume = 5 | issue = 1 | pages = 3–11 | doi = 10.1080/17439760903271116 | s2cid = 143476331 }}</ref>

==Applications==

===Applications suggested by Csíkszentmihályi versus other practitioners===
Only Csíkszentmihályi seems to have published suggestions for [[extrinsic]] applications of the flow concept, such as [[design]] methods for playgrounds to elicit the flow experience. Other practitioners of Csíkszentmihályi's flow concept focus on [[intrinsic and extrinsic properties (philosophy)|intrinsic]] applications, such as [[spirituality]], [[performance improvement]], or [[self-help]]. His work has also informed the measurement of donor momentum by The New Science of Philanthropy.

===Education===
[[File:South-Goodwin.jpg|thumb|250px|Young child, painting a model]]
In [[education]], the concept of [[overlearning]] plays a role in a student's ability to achieve flow. Csíkszentmihályi<ref name= "Csikszentmihályi_1990" /> states that overlearning enables the mind to concentrate on visualizing the desired performance as a singular, integrated action instead of a set of actions. Challenging assignments that (slightly) stretch one's skills lead to flow.<ref name=PP11>{{citation | vauthors = Snyder CR, Lopez SJ |name-list-style=amp  |author-link1=C. R. Snyder |year=2007 |title=Positive Psychology |publisher=Sage Publications, Inc. |chapter=11 |isbn=978-0-7619-2633-7}}</ref>

In the 1950s British cybernetician Gordon Pask designed an adaptive teaching machine called SAKI, an early example of "e-learning". The machine is discussed in some detail in Stafford Beer's book "Cybernetics and Management".<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Beer S |year=1967 |title=Cybernetics and Management |publisher=The English Universities Press |isbn=978-0340045947}}</ref> In the patent application for SAKI (1956),<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Pask G |title=US patent 2984017 A: Apparatus for assisting an operator in performing a skill |url=https://www.google.com/patents/US2984017}}</ref> Pask's comments (some of which are included below) indicate an awareness of the pedagogical importance of balancing student competence with didactic challenge, which is quite consistent with flow theory:

{{blockquote|If the operator is receiving data at too slow a rate, he is likely to become bored and attend to other irrelevant data.

If the data given indicates too precisely what responses the operator is required to make, the skill becomes too easy to perform and the operator again tends to become bored.

If the data given is too complicated or is given at too great a rate, the operator is unable to deal with it. He is then liable to become discouraged and lose interest in performing or learning the skill.

Ideally, for an operator to perform a skill efficiently, the data presented to him should always be of sufficient complexity to maintain his interest and maintain a competitive situation, but not so complex as to discourage the operator. Similarly these conditions should obtain at each stage of a learning process if it is to be efficient. A tutor teaching one pupil seeks to maintain just these conditions.}}

Around 2000, it came to the attention of Csíkszentmihályi that the principles and practices of the [[Montessori Method]] of education seemed to purposefully set up continuous flow opportunities and experiences for students. Csíkszentmihályi and psychologist Kevin Rathunde embarked on a multi-year study of student experiences in Montessori settings and traditional educational settings. The research supported observations that students achieved flow experiences more frequently in Montessori settings.<ref>{{citation | vauthors = Rathunde K, Csikszetnmihályi M |journal=American Journal of Education |title=Middle school students' motivation and quality of experience: A comparison of Montessori and traditional school environments |issue=3 |volume=111 |pages=341–371 |year=2005 |doi=10.1086/428885|s2cid=146766889 }}</ref><ref>{{citation | vauthors = Rathunde K, Csikszentmihályi M |author-link2=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi |title=The social context of middle school: Teachers, friends, and activities in Montessori and traditional school environments |journal=Elementary School Journal |volume=106 |issue=1 |pages=59–79 |year=2005 |doi=10.1086/496907|s2cid=145336225 }}</ref><ref>{{cite conference | vauthors = Rathunde K, Csikszentmihályi M |author-link2=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi |title=The developing person: An experiential perspective | veditors = Lerner RM, Damon W |series=Handbook of Child Psychology |book-title=Theoretical models of human development |issue=1 |edition=6 |place=New York |publisher=Wiley |year=2006}}</ref>

===Music===
Musicians, especially [[Musical improvisation|improvisational]] [[solo (music)|soloists]], may experience a state of flow while playing their instrument.<ref>{{citation | vauthors = Parncutt R, McPherson GE |title=The Science & Psychology of Music Performance: Creative Strategies for Teaching and Learning Book |publisher=Oxford University Press US |page=119 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NCySp2NzAm8C&q=improvisation+flow+Cs%C3%ADkszentmih%C3%A1lyi&pg=PA119 |access-date=2009-02-07 |year=2002 |isbn=978-0-19-513810-8}}</ref>
Research has shown that performers in a flow state have a heightened quality of performance as opposed to when they are not in a flow state.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=O'Neill|first=Susan|date=1999|title=Flow Theory and the Development of Musical Performance Skills|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/40318998|journal=Bulletin of the Council for Research in Music Education|volume=141|issue=141|pages=129–134|jstor=40318998|via=University of Illinois Press}}</ref> In a study performed with professional classical pianists who played piano pieces several times to induce a flow state, a significant relationship was found between the flow state of the pianist and the pianist's heart rate, blood pressure, and major facial muscles. As the pianist entered the flow state, heart rate and blood pressure decreased, and the major facial muscles relaxed. This study further emphasized that flow is a state of effortless attention. In spite of the effortless attention and overall relaxation of the body, the performance of the pianist during the flow state improved.<ref name="de ManzanoTheorell2010">{{cite journal | vauthors = de Manzano O, Theorell T, Harmat L, Ullén F | title = The psychophysiology of flow during piano playing | journal = Emotion | volume = 10 | issue = 3 | pages = 301–311 | date = June 2010 | pmid = 20515220 | doi = 10.1037/a0018432 }}</ref>

Groups of drummers go through a state of flow when they sense a collective energy that drives the beat, something they refer to as ''getting into the groove'' or [[Entrainment (biomusicology)|entrainment]]. Likewise, drummers and bass guitarists often describe a state of flow when they are feeling the downbeat together as being ''in the pocket.''<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Elliott B | date = 9 June 2003 |title=In The Pocket |website=www.drummercafe.com |language=en-GB |url=http://www.drummercafe.com/education/articles/in-the-pocket.html |access-date=2016-04-01}}</ref> Researchers have measured flow through subscales; challenge-skill balance, merging of action and awareness, clear goals, unambiguous feedback, total concentration, sense of control, loss of self-consciousness, transformation of time and autotelic experience.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wrigley WJ, Emmerson SB | title = The experience of the flow state in live music performance. | journal = Psychology of Music | date = May 2013 | volume = 41 | issue = 3 | pages = 292–305 | doi = 10.1177/0305735611425903 | s2cid = 144877389 }}=</ref>

===Sports===
[[File:Concentration.jpg|thumb|Flow may occur in challenging sports such as [[eventing]].]]
The concept of ''being in the zone'' during an athletic performance fit within Csíkszentmihályi's description of the flow experience, and theories and applications of ''being in the zone'' and its relationship with an athletic competitive advantage are topics studied in the field of [[sport psychology]].<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Young JA, Pain MD |title=The Zone: Evidence of a Universal Phenomenon for Athletes Across Sports |work=Athletic Insight |url=http://www.athleticinsight.com/Vol1Iss3/Empirical_Zone.htm |access-date=2008-05-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180129145759/http://www.athleticinsight.com/Vol1Iss3/Empirical_Zone.htm |archive-date=2018-01-29 |url-status=dead }}</ref>

[[Timothy Gallwey]]'s influential works on the "inner game" of sports such as [[golf]] and [[tennis]] described the mental coaching and attitudes required to "get in the zone" and fully internalize mastery of the sport.<ref>{{citation | vauthors = Galwey T |year=1976 |title=Inner Tennis&nbsp;— Playing the Game}}</ref>

Roy Palmer suggests that "being in the zone" may also influence movement patterns as better integration of the conscious and subconscious reflex functions improves coordination. Many athletes describe the effortless nature of their performance while achieving personal bests.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = King S |year=2006 |title=Running in the Zone A Handbook for Seasoned Athletes |publisher=Trafford Publishing |location=Victoria, BC Canada |isbn=978-1412068574}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Locke A |date=2001-02-01 |title=Being in the zone: notions of agency in athletic performance |journal=Proceedings of the British Psychological Society |issn=1350-472X |volume=9 |issue=1 |page=83 |url=http://eprints.hud.ac.uk/7223/ |access-date=2016-04-01}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Jenkins L |date=May 20, 2015 |title=Stephen Curry's next stage: MVP has Warriors closing in on the NBA Finals |website=www.si.com |url=https://www.si.com/nba/2015/05/21/stephen-curry-mvp-golden-state-warriors-sports-illustrated-nba-playoffs |access-date=2016-04-01}}</ref>

In many [[martial arts]], the term [[Budō]] is used to describe psychological flow.<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Henderson S |date=November 24, 2015 |title=Finding Your Inner Master – Kigatsuku |website=International Budo Institute |url=http://www.i-budo.com/zen-kata/ |access-date=21 September 2017}}</ref> [[Mixed martial arts]] champion and [[Karate]] master [[Lyoto Machida]] uses meditation techniques before fights to attain [[mushin (mental state)|mushin]], a concept that, by his description, is in all respects equal to flow.

The [[Formula One]] driver [[Ayrton Senna]], during qualifying for the [[1988 Monaco Grand Prix]], explained: "I was already on pole, [...] and I just kept going. Suddenly I was nearly two seconds faster than anybody else, including my team mate with the same car. And suddenly I realised that I was no longer driving the car consciously. I was driving it by a kind of instinct, only I was in a different dimension. It was like I was in a tunnel."<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Donaldson G |date=May 23, 2019 |title=Senna's Magic Monaco Lap |url=http://www.f1speedwriter.com/2018/05/sennas-magic-monaco-lap.html |access-date=2020-12-23}}</ref>

Former [[Grand Prix motorcycle racing|500 GP]] rider [[Wayne Gardner]] talking about his victory at the [[1990 Australian motorcycle Grand Prix|1990 Australian Grand Prix]] on ''The Unrideables 2'' documentary said: "During these last five laps I had this sort of above body experience where actually raised up above and I could see myself racing. It was kind of a remote control and it's the weirdest thing I've ever had in my life. [...]" After the race [[Mick Doohan|Mick [Doohan]]] and in fact [[Wayne Rainey]] said: "How the hell did you do that?" and I said: "I have no idea."<ref>{{cite web |title=The Unrideables 2 |url=https://vimeo.com/92724437#t=1212s}}</ref>

===Religion and spirituality===
In [[Yoga|yogic]] traditions such as [[Raja Yoga]], reference is made to a state of ''flow''<ref>{{cite web |title=Yoga Sutras 3.9-3.16: Witnessing Subtle Transitions with Samyama |publisher=Swamij.com |url=http://www.swamij.com/yoga-sutras-30916.htm |access-date=2013-11-12}}</ref> in the practice of [[Samyama]], a psychological absorption in the object of meditation.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Sansonese JN |author-link=J. Nigro Sansonese |year=1994 |title=The Body of Myth: Mythology, Shamanic Trance, and the Sacred Geography of the Body |publisher=Inner Traditions |isbn=978-0-89281-409-1 |page=26 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aUZBqTKTAfIC&q=samyama+japan+koan&pg=PA26 |access-date=2009-03-06}}</ref>

===Games and gaming===

Flow in [[game]]s and gaming has been linked to the [[laws of learning]] as a part of the explanation for why learning-games (the use of games to introduce material, improve understanding, or increase retention) have the potential to be effective.<ref name= "Murphy_2011" >{{citation | vauthors = Murphy C |date=August 2011 |title=Why Games Work and the Science of Learning |url=http://www.goodgamesbydesign.com/Files/WhyGamesWork_TheScienceOfLearning_CMurphy_2011.pdf |access-date=October 7, 2021}}</ref>{{failed verification|date=October 2021|reason=Author claims on personal site to have intended to present at the 'Modsim World 2011' conference but the proceedings website for that shows no such paper or mention of author. I also found a claim it won an 'Best Tutorial' at I/ITSEC 2011 but again the proceedings webpage shows no such paper or mention of the author.}} In particular, flow is intrinsically motivating, which is a part of the law of readiness. The condition of feedback, required for flow, is associated with the feedback aspects of the law of exercise. This is exhibited in well designed games, in particular, where players perform at the edge of their competency as they are guided by clear goals and feedback.<ref name="Rutledge_2012"/> The positive emotions associated with flow are associated with the law of effect. The intense experiences of being in a state of flow are directly associated with the law of intensity. Thus, the experience of gaming can be so engaging and motivating as it meets many of the laws of learning, which are inextricably connected to creating flow.

In games often much can be achieved thematically through an imbalance between challenge level and skill level. Horror games often keep challenges significantly above the player's level of competency in order to foster a continual feeling of anxiety. Conversely, so called "relaxation games" keep the level of challenges significantly below the player's competency level, in order to achieve an opposite effect.{{citation needed|date=January 2016}} The video game ''[[Flow (video game)|Flow]]'' was designed as part of [[Jenova Chen]]'s master's thesis for exploring the design decisions that allow players to achieve the flow state, by adjusting the difficulty dynamically during play.<ref name="Miller_2006">{{cite web | vauthors = Miller R |date=2006-09-18 |title=Joystiq interview: Jenova Chen |publisher=[[Joystiq]] |url=http://www.joystiq.com/2006/09/18/joystiq-qanda-jenova-chen/ |access-date=2011-01-05 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090102012220/http://www.joystiq.com/2006/09/18/joystiq-qanda-jenova-chen/ |archive-date=2009-01-02}}</ref>

It improves performance; calling the phenomenon "TV trance," a 1981 ''[[BYTE]]'' article discussed how "the best seem to enter a trance where they play but don't pay attention to the details of the game."<ref name="Smith_1981">{{cite news | vauthors = Smith T |date=December 1981 |title=The Game of Left/Right |work=BYTE |page=278 |url=https://archive.org/stream/byte-magazine-1981-12/1981_12_BYTE_06-12_Computer_Games#page/n279/mode/2up |access-date=29 January 2015}}</ref> The primary goal of games is to create entertainment through [[Motivation#Types of motivation|intrinsic motivation]], which is related to flow; that is, without intrinsic motivation it is virtually impossible to establish flow.<ref name="Lowry_2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Lowry PB, Gaskin J, Twyman N, Hammer B, Roberts T | title = Taking 'fun and games' seriously: Proposing the hedonic-motivation system adoption model (HMSAM) | journal = Journal of the Association for Information Systems | date = November 2012 | volume = 14 | issue = 11 | pages = 617–71 | doi = 10.17705/1jais.00347 | ssrn = 2177442 | s2cid = 2810596 | url = https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2177442 }}</ref> Through the balance of skill and challenge, the player's brain is aroused, with attention engaged and motivation high.<ref name="Rutledge_2012">{{cite web | vauthors = Rutledge P |title=The Positive Side of Video Games: Part III |publisher=paper blog |url=http://en.paperblog.com/the-positive-side-of-video-games-part-iii-294723/ |access-date=2012-11-28}}</ref> Thus, the use of flow in games helps foster an enjoyable experience, which in turn increases motivation and draws players to continue playing. As such, game designers strive to integrate flow principles into their projects.<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Chen J |title=Flow in Games |url=http://www.jenovachen.com/flowingames/introduction.htm |access-date=2008-05-16 |year=2008}}</ref> Overall, the experience of play is fluid and is intrinsically psychologically rewarding independent of scores or in-game successes in the flow state.<ref name="Rutledge_2012"/>

===Design of intrinsically motivated computer systems===
A simplified modification to flow has been combined with the [[technology acceptance model]] (TAM) to help guide the design of and explain the adoption of intrinsically motivated computer systems. This model, the hedonic-motivation system adoption model (HMSAM) is modelled to improve the understanding of hedonic-motivation systems (HMS) adoption.<ref name="Lowry_2012"/> HMS are systems used primarily to fulfill users' intrinsic motivations, such for online gaming, virtual worlds, online shopping, learning/education, online dating, digital music repositories, social networking, online pornography, gamified systems, and for general gamification. Instead of a minor, TAM extension, HMSAM is an HMS-specific system acceptance model based on an alternative theoretical perspective, which is in turn grounded in flow-based concept of cognitive absorption (CA). The HMSAM further builds on van der Heijden's (2004) model of hedonic system adoption<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Van der Heijden H | date = 2004 | title = User Acceptance of Hedonic Information Systems | journal = MIS Quarterly | volume = 28 | issue = 4 | pages = 695–704 | doi = 10.2307/25148660 | jstor = 25148660 }}</ref> by including CA as a key mediator of perceived ease of use (PEOU) and of behavioral intentions to use (BIU) hedonic-motivation systems. Typically, models simplistically represent "intrinsic motivations" by mere perceived enjoyed. Instead, HMSAM uses the more complex, rich construct of CA, which includes joy, control, curiosity, focused immersion, and temporal dissociation. CA is construct that is grounded in the seminal flow literature, yet CA has traditionally been used as a static construct, as if all five of its subconstructs occur at the same time—in direct contradiction to the flow literature. Thus, part of HMSAM's contribution is to return CA closer to its flow roots by re-ordering these CA subconstructs into more natural process-variance order as predicted by flow. Empirical data collection along with mediation tests further support this modeling approach.

==Professions and work==
Developers of computer software reference getting into a flow state as "wired in", or sometimes as ''The Zone'',<ref>{{citation
 | vauthors = Lopp M 
 |date = 12 June 2007
 |title = Managing Humans: Biting and Humorous Tales of a Software Engineering Manager
 |chapter = Chapter 25: A Nerd in a Cave
 |publisher = [[Apress]]
 |isbn = 978-1-59059-844-3
 |page=143
 |chapter-url = http://www.randsinrepose.com/archives/2006/07/10/a_nerd_in_a_cave.html
 |quote=[The Zone] is a deeply creative space where inspiration is built. Anything which you perceive as beautiful, useful, or fun comes from someone stumbling through The Zone.
}}</ref><ref>{{citation
 | vauthors = Spolsky J |author-link=Joel Spolsky
 |date = 9 August 2000
 |title = The Joel Test: 12 Steps to Better Code
 |url = http://www.joelonsoftware.com/articles/fog0000000043.html
 |quote = We all know that knowledge workers work best by getting into 'flow', also known as being 'in the zone' (...) Writers, programmers, scientists, and even basketball players will tell you about being in the zone.
 }}</ref> ''hack mode'',<ref>{{cite web
 |date = 12 November 2013
 |title = Hack Mode
 |publisher = [[Jargon File]]
 |url = http://catb.org/jargon/html/H/hack-mode.html
}}</ref> or operating on ''software time''<ref>{{citation
 | vauthors = Rosenberg S |author-link=Scott Rosenberg (journalist)
 |year = 2007
 |title = Dreaming in Code: Two Dozen Programmers, Three Years, 4,732 Bugs, and One Quest for Transcendent Software
 |url = http://www.dreamingincode.com/book-excerpt/
|quote = When things go well, you can lose track of passing hours in the state psychologists call "flow." When things go badly, you get stuck, frozen between dimensions, unable to move or see a way forward. Either way, you've left the clock far behind. You're on software time.
}}</ref> when developing in an undistracted state. Stock market operators often use the term "in the pipe" to describe the psychological state of flow when trading during high volume days and market corrections. Professional [[poker]] players use the term "playing the A-game" when referring to the state of highest concentration and strategical awareness, while [[pool (cue sports)|pool]] players often call the state being in "dead stroke".

===In the workplace===
Conditions of flow, defined as a state in which challenges and skills are equally matched, play an extremely important role in the workplace.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wang |first1=Xiaochen |last2=Shaheryar |date=2020 |title=Work-Related Flow: The Development of a Theoretical Framework Based on the High Involvement HRM Practices With Mediating Role of Affective Commitment and Moderating Effect of Emotional Intelligence |journal=Frontiers in Psychology |volume=11 |page=564444 |doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2020.564444 |pmid=33414741 |pmc=7784544 |issn=1664-1078|doi-access=free }}</ref> Because flow is associated with achievement, its development may have specific implications for increased workplace satisfaction and achievement. Flow researchers, such as Csikszentmihályi, believe that certain interventions may be performed to enhance and increase flow in the workplace, through which people would gain 'intrinsic rewards that encourage persistence" and provide benefits. In his consultation work, Csikszentmihályi emphasizes finding activities and environments that are conducive to flow, and then identifying and developing personal characteristics to increase experiences of flow. Applying these methods in the workplace can improve morale by fostering a sense of greater happiness and accomplishment, which may be correlated with increased performance. In his review of Mihály Csikszentmihályi's book "Good Business: Leadership, Flow, and the Making of Meaning," Coert Visser introduces the ideas presented by Csikszentmihályi, including "good work" in which one "enjoys doing your best while at the same time contributing to something beyond yourself."<ref name=Visser_2007>{{cite web | vauthors = Visser C |title=Good Business: Leadership, Flow, and the Making of Meaning |url=http://articlescoertvisser.blogspot.com/2007/11/good-business-leadership-flow-and.html |access-date=26 September 2012 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131110043540/http://articlescoertvisser.blogspot.com/2007/11/good-business-leadership-flow-and.html |archive-date=10 November 2013}}</ref> He then provides tools by which managers and employees can create an atmosphere that encourages good work. Some consultants suggest that the experience sampling form (EMS) method be used for individuals and teams in the workplace in order to identify how time is currently being spent, and where focus should be redirected to in order to maximize flow experiences.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Foster S, Lloyd P |date=2007 |title=Positive Psychology Principles Applied to Consulting Psychology at the Individual and Group Level |journal=Consulting Psychology Journal: Practice and Research |doi=10.1037/1065-9293.59.1.30 |volume=59 |issue=1 |pages=30–40}}</ref>

In order to achieve flow, Csikszentmihályi lays out the following three conditions:
* Goals are clear
* Feedback is immediate
* A balance exists between opportunity and capacity

Csikszentmihályi argues that with increased experiences of flow, people experience "growth towards complexity". People flourish as their achievements grow and with that comes development of increasing "emotional, cognitive, and social complexity."<ref name=Visser_2007/> Creating a workplace atmosphere that allows for flow and growth, Csikszentmihályi argues, can increase the happiness and achievement of employees. An increasingly popular way of promoting greater flow in the workplace is using the "[[Serious Play|serious play]]" facilitation methods. Some commercial organisations have used the concept of flow in building corporate branding and identity, for example, The Floow Limited, which created its company brand from the concept.

===Barriers===
There are, however, barriers to achieving flow in the workplace. In his chapter "Why Flow Doesn't Happen on the Job," Csikszentmihályi argues the first reason that flow does not occur is that the goals of one's job are not clear. He explains that while some tasks at work may fit into a larger, organization plan, the individual worker may not see where their individual task fits it. Second, limited feedback about one's work can reduce motivation and leaves the employee unaware of whether or not they did a good job. When there is little communication of feedback, an employee may not be assigned tasks that challenge them or seem important, which could potentially prevent an opportunity for flow.

In the study "Predicting flow at work: Investigating the activities and job characteristics that predict flow states at work", Karina Nielsen and Bryan Cleal used a 9-item flow scale to examine predictors of flow at two levels: activity level (such as brainstorming, problem solving, and evaluation) and at a more stable level (such as role clarity, influence, and cognitive demands). They found that activities such as planning, problem solving, and evaluation predicted transient flow states, but that more stable job characteristics were not found to predict flow at work. This study can help us identify which task at work can be cultivated and emphasized in order to help employees experience flow on the job.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Nielsen K, Cleal B | title = Predicting flow at work: investigating the activities and job characteristics that predict flow states at work | journal = Journal of Occupational Health Psychology | volume = 15 | issue = 2 | pages = 180–190 | date = April 2010 | pmid = 20364915 | doi = 10.1037/a0018893 }}</ref> In her article in ''Positive Psychology News Daily'', Kathryn Britton examines the importance of experiencing flow in the workplace beyond the individual benefits it creates. She writes, "Flow isn't just valuable to individuals; it also contributes to organizational goals. For example, frequent experiences of flow at work lead to higher productivity, innovation, and employee development (Csikszentmihályi, 1991, 2004). So finding ways to increase the frequency of flow experiences can be one way for people to work together to increase the effectiveness of their workplaces."<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Britton K |date=7 September 2008 |title=Flowing Together |work=Positive Psychology News Daily |url=http://positivepsychologynews.com/news/kathryn-britton/200809071013}}</ref>

==Outcomes==

===Positive experiences===
Books by Csikszentmihályi suggest that enhancing the time spent in flow makes our lives more happy and successful. Flow experiences are predicted to lead to positive affect as well as to better performance.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_1990" /><ref name = "Csíkszentmihályi_1996" /> For example, delinquent behavior was reduced in adolescents after two years of enhancing flow through activities.<ref name = "Nakamura_2005" />

People who have experienced flow, describe the following feelings:
# Completely involved in what we are doing – focused, concentrated.
# A sense of ecstasy – of being outside everyday reality.
# Great inner clarity – knowing what needs to be done, and how well we are doing.
# Knowing that the activity is doable – that our skills are adequate to the task.
# A sense of serenity – no worries about oneself, and a feeling of growing beyond the boundaries of the ego.
# Timelessness – thoroughly focused on the present, hours seem to pass by the minute.
# Intrinsic motivation – whatever produces flow becomes its own reward.<ref>{{citation | vauthors = Csikszentmihalyi M |author-link=Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi |title=Flow, the secret to happiness |date=23 October 2008 |url=https://www.ted.com/talks/mihaly_csikszentmihalyi_on_flow#t-883527 |access-date=2017-12-02}}</ref>
However, further empirical evidence is required to substantiate these preliminary indications, as flow researchers continue to explore the problem of how to directly investigate causal consequences of flow experiences using modern scientific instrumentation to observe the neuro-physiological correlates of the flow state.<ref name="Landhäußer_2012">{{cite book | vauthors = Landhäußer A, Keller J | chapter = Flow and its affective, cognitive, and performance-related consequences. | veditors = Engeser S | title = Advances in flow research | date = 2012 | pages = 65–85 | publisher = Springer | location = New York, NY. | doi = 10.1007/978-1-4614-2359-1_4 | isbn = 978-1-4614-2359-1 }}</ref>

====Positive affect and life satisfaction====
Flow is an innately positive experience; it is known to "produce intense feelings of enjoyment".<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_1998"/> An experience that is so enjoyable should lead to positive [[affect (psychology)|affect]] and [[happiness]] in the long run. Also, Csikszentmihályi stated that happiness is derived from personal development and growth&nbsp;– and flow situations permit the experience of personal development.<ref name = "Csíkszentmihályi_1996" />

Several studies found that flow experiences and positive affect go hand in hand,<ref name="Schüler_2007"/><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rheinberg F, Manig Y, Kliegl R, Engeser S, Vollmeyer R | title = Flow bei der Arbeit, doch Glück in der Freizeit: Zielausrichtung, Flow und Glücksgefühle. | trans-title = Flow during work but happiness during leisure time: goals, flow-experience, and happiness | language = German | journal = Zeitschrift für Arbeits-und Organisationspsychologie A&O | date = July 2007 | volume = 51 | issue = 3 | pages = 105–15 | doi = 10.1026/0932-4089.51.3.105 }}</ref> and that challenges and skills above the individual's average foster positive affect.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Clarke SG, Haworth JT | title =  'Flow' experience in the daily lives of sixth‐form college students. | journal = British Journal of Psychology | date = November 1994 | volume = 85 | issue = 4 | pages = 511–23 | doi =10.1111/j.2044-8295.1994.tb02538.x }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Massimini F, Carli M | date = 1988 | chapter = The systematic assessment of flow in daily experience. | veditors = Csikszentmihályi M, Csikszentmihályi IS | title = Optimal Experience: Psychological Studies of Flow in Consciousness | pages = 288–306 | location = New York | publisher = Cambridge University Press }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Shernoff DJ, Csikszentmihalyi M, Schneider B, Shernoff ES | chapter = Student engagement in high school classrooms from the perspective of flow theory.  | title = Applications of flow in human development and education | date = 2014 | pages = 475–494 | publisher = Springer | location = Dordrecht. }}</ref> However, the causal processes underlying those relationships remain unclear at present.

====Performance and learning====
Flow experiences imply a growth principle. When one is in a flow state, they are working to master the activity at hand. To maintain that flow state, one must seek increasingly greater challenges. Attempting these new, difficult challenges stretches one's skills. One emerges from such a flow experience with a bit of personal growth and great "feelings of competence and efficacy".<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_2005"/> By increasing time spent in flow, intrinsic motivation and self-directed learning also increases.<ref>{{cite conference | vauthors = Hektner JM, Csikszentmihalyi M | title = A longitudinal exploration of flow and intrinsic motivation in adolescents | conference = Annual meeting of the American education research association | location = New York | publisher = Alfred Sloan Foundation. | url = https://eric.ed.gov/?id=ED395261 }}</ref>

Flow has a documented correlation with high performance in the fields of artistic and scientific creativity,<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Perry SJ | date = 1999 | title = Writing in Flow | location = Cincinnati, OH | publisher = Writer’s Digest Books }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Sawyer K | title = Improvisational creativity: An analysis of jazz performance. | journal = Creativity Research Journal | date = January 1992 | volume = 5 | issue = 3 | pages = 253–63 | doi = 10.1080/10400419209534439 }}</ref> teaching,<ref name = "Csíkszentmihályi_1996">{{cite book | vauthors = Csíkszentmihályi M | date = 1996 | title = Finding Flow: The Psychology of Engagement with Everyday Life | publisher = Basic Books | isbn = 0-465-02411-4 }}</ref> learning,<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Csikszentmihalyi, M, Rathunde K, Whalen S | date = 1993 | title = Talented teenagers: The roots of success and failure. | location = New York | publisher = Cambridge University Press }}</ref> and sports;<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Jackson SA, Thomas PR, Marsh HW, Smethurst CJ | date = 2001 | title = Relationship between flow, self-concept, psychological skills, and performance. | journal = Journal of Applied Sport Psychology | volume = 13 | issue = 2 | pages = 129–153 | doi = 10.1080/104132001753149865 | s2cid = 142097761 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stein GL, Kimiecik JC, Daniels J, Jackson SA | title = Psychological antecedents of flow in recreational sport. | journal = Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin | date = February 1995 | volume = 21 | issue = 2 | pages = 125–135 | doi = 10.1177/0146167295212003 | s2cid = 146150033 }}</ref>

Flow has been linked to persistence and achievement in activities while also helping to lower anxiety during various activities and raise self-esteem.<ref name = "Nakamura_2005" />

However, evidence regarding better performance in flow situations is mixed.<ref name="Landhäußer_2012"/> For sure, the association between the two is a reciprocal one. That is, flow experiences may foster better performance but, on the other hand, good performance makes flow experiences more likely. Results of a longitudinal study in the academic context indicate that the causal effect of flow on performance is only of small magnitude and the strong relationship between the two is driven by an effect of performance on flow.<ref name="Engeser_2008"/>
In the long run, flow experiences in a specific activity may lead to higher performance in that activity as flow is positively correlated with a higher subsequent motivation to perform and to perform well.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_2005"/>

==Criticism==
{{expand section|date=November 2019}}
Csikszentmihályi writes about the dangers of flow himself: <blockquote>...enjoyable activities that produce flow have a potentially negative effect: while they are capable of improving the quality of existence by creating order in the mind, they can become addictive, at which point the self becomes captive of a certain kind of order, and is then unwilling to cope with the ambiguities of life.</blockquote>
Further, he writes:<blockquote>
The flow experience, like everything else, is not "good" in an absolute sense. It is good only in that it has the potential to make life more rich, intense, and meaningful; it is good because it increases the strengths and complexity of the self. But whether the consequence of any particular instance of flow is good in a larger sense needs to be discussed and evaluated in terms of more inclusive social criteria.<ref name="Csikszentmihályi_2013">{{cite book | vauthors = Csikszentmihályi M |year=1992 |title=Flow: The Psychology of Happiness |publisher=Rider |isbn=978-0-7126-5477-7 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_qLWNQAACAAJ |access-date=25 October 2015}}</ref></blockquote>

Keller and Landhäußer (2012, p.&nbsp;56) advocate for a flow intensity model because many models of flow have trouble predicting the intensity of flow experiences that can occur under various circumstances where skill and task demands fit together to produce flow.<ref name="Keller_2012"/>

Cowley et al. found that because self-reported flow happens after-the-fact, it does not really capture the aspect of flow that happens in the moment. Furthermore, that aspect of flow is prone to change, so the self-reported experience of flow cannot be trusted as much.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cowley BU, Palomäki J, Tammi T, Frantsi R, Inkilä VP, Lehtonen N, Pölönen P, Vepsäläinen J, Lappi O | display-authors = 6 | title = Flow Experiences During Visuomotor Skill Acquisition Reflect Deviation From a Power-Law Learning Curve, but Not Overall Level of Skill | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 10 | pages = 1126 | date = 2019-05-15 | pmid = 31156519 | pmc = 6530424 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2019.01126 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Cameron et al. found that there is not a lot of information on group flow, and this may be hindering development in managerial and theoretical contributions.<ref name="Léger_2013">{{cite conference | vauthors = Léger PM, Sénécal S, Aubé C, Cameron AF, de Guinea AO, Brunelle E |title=The Influence of Group Flow on Group Performance: a Research Program | conference =  Proceedings of the Gmunden Retreat on NeuroIS | date = June 2013 | page = 13 |url= https://www.researchgate.net/publication/252931290}}</ref>

==Future directions==

Cameron et al. proposed a research program that focuses on how group flow is different from individual flow, and how group flow affects group performance. These ideas will address some of the issues in group flow research such as poor data collection and interpretation.<ref name="Léger_2013"/>

Sridhar & Lyngdoh suggested that research should investigate how mobility affects the ethical performance of sales professionals. Furthermore, there should be longitudinal studies done in various fields to understand the ethical implications of flow in sales.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Sridhar G, Lyngdoh T |date=2019-09-01 |title=Flow and Information Sharing as Predictors of Ethical Selling Behavior |journal=Journal of Business Ethics |issn=1573-0697 |doi=10.1007/s10551-017-3743-8 |s2cid=158513460 |volume=158 |issue=3 |pages=807–823 |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-017-3743-8}}</ref>

From their study, Chen et al. found that there needs to be more research done on how competition affects game-based learning.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chen CH, Law V, Huang K |date=2019-08-01 |title=The roles of engagement and competition on learner's performance and motivation in game-based science learning |journal=Educational Technology Research and Development |issn=1556-6501 |doi=10.1007/s11423-019-09670-7 |s2cid=182265497 |volume=67 |issue=4 |pages=1003–1024 |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s11423-019-09670-7}}</ref>

Linden et al. suggest that a neuroscientific model of flow would lead to new research questions that would guide future discoveries, experiments, and less obvious questions.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = van der Linden D, Tops M, Bakker AB | title = Go with the flow: A neuroscientific view on being fully engaged | journal = The European Journal of Neuroscience | volume = 53 | issue = 4 | pages = 947–963 | date = February 2021 | pmid = 33084102 | pmc = 7983950 | doi = 10.1111/ejn.15014 }}</ref>

Thissen et al. propose that more research is recommended in 2020 to understand how traffic affects fiction reading for all types of readers.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Thissen BA, Menninghaus W, Schlotz W |date=2020 |title=The pleasures of reading fiction explained by flow, presence, identification, suspense, and cognitive involvement. |journal=Psychology of Aesthetics |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=710–724 |via=APA PsycNet |doi=10.1037/aca0000367|s2cid=229432873 }}</ref>

== References ==
===Citations===
{{Reflist|colwidth=30em}}

===Sources===
{{refbegin}}
* {{citation | vauthors = Csíkszentmihályi M |title=Creativity: Flow and the Psychology of Discovery and Invention |place=New York |publisher=Harper Perennial |year=1996 |isbn=978-0-06-092820-9}}
* {{citation | vauthors = Csíkszentmihályi M |title=Finding Flow: The Psychology of Engagement With Everyday Life |publisher=Basic Books |year=1996 |isbn=978-0-465-02411-7}} (a popular exposition emphasizing technique)
* {{citation | vauthors = Csíkszentmihályi M |title=Good Business: Leadership, Flow, and the Making of Meaning |year=2003 |place=New York |isbn=978-0-14-200409-8 |publisher=Penguin Books}}
* {{Citation | vauthors = Egbert J |title=A Study of Flow Theory in the Foreign Language Classroom |journal=The Modern Language Journal |volume=87 |issue=4 |pages=499–518 |year=2003 |doi=10.1111/1540-4781.00204}}
* {{citation | vauthors = Jackson SA, Csíkszentmihályi M |title=Flow in Sports: The Keys to Optimal Experiences and Performances |place=Champaign, Illinois |publisher=Human Kinetics Publishers |isbn=978-0-88011-876-7 |year=1999}}
* {{Citation | vauthors = Mainemelis C |title=When the Muse Takes It All: A Model for the Experience of Timelessness in Organizations |journal=The Academy of Management Review |volume=26 |issue=4 |pages=548–565 |year=2001 |doi=10.2307/3560241 |jstor=3560241}}
* {{Citation | vauthors = Shainberg L |title=Finding 'The Zone' |newspaper=The New York Times Magazine |date=1989-04-09 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1989/04/09/magazine/finding-the-zone.html }}
{{refend}}
